items.t: Trump says US and Iran to hold fresh talks in Qatar after weekend clashes
items.sum: President Trump said US and Iranian representatives would meet in Doha on Tuesday, following a weekend exchange of strikes that strained the fragile ceasefire. A standdown was agreed Sunday evening, with technical talks now set to test whether the truce can hold.
items.mkt: This is the regime-changer in reverse: a return to the table pulls the conflict back from open war and lets the weekend war premium bleed out of oil and gold.

items.t: Strait of Hormuz ceasefire holds as US and Iran agree to Doha talks after five days of strikes
items.sum: After five days of strikes, Washington and Tehran agreed a standdown and scheduled talks in Doha, with Qatar mediating over roughly $6 billion in frozen Iranian funds. Tehran insists the interim deal must be implemented before final negotiations begin.
items.mkt: Whether Doha produces written clarity on which transit corridor commercial vessels may use is now the single biggest swing factor for the oil risk premium.

items.t: US, Iran peace talks in Doha off to a shaky start
items.sum: The Doha session opened on uncertain footing, with an Iranian Foreign Ministry spokesperson saying no meeting with the American side had been scheduled and that dialogue with mediator Qatar over the interim deal was more likely. The ambiguity leaves the diplomatic track hanging by a thread.
items.mkt: A clean break in talks would snap a large war premium back into oil and gold within hours, so headline risk around Doha is acute and two-sided.

items.t: Oil falls ~1% to $72.4 as markets await clarity on US-Iran talks in Doha
items.sum: Brent crude slipped about 1% to roughly $72.4 a barrel as traders positioned for a possible diplomatic breakthrough, while Gulf transits through the Strait of Hormuz stayed consistent. The move unwinds part of the premium built during the weekend escalation.
items.mkt: With the tape now pricing diplomacy rather than war, oil is biased lower toward the high $60s on any concrete Doha progress, but a failed session reopens the upside gap.

items.t: Gold slips toward $4,030, on track for a fourth straight monthly loss
items.sum: Gold fell to around $4,030-$4,040 an ounce as uncertainty over the US-Iran talks stoked inflation concerns and reinforced expectations of Fed rate hikes. Bullion is down more than 10% on the month, its fourth straight monthly decline, and sits far below its January record near $5,600.
items.mkt: Monetary policy still trumps geopolitics for gold; only a genuine Hormuz/Iran re-escalation can force a haven bid straight through the hawkish-Fed headwind.

items.t: Fed holds at 3.50-3.75% but dot plot flips to a 2026 hike under new chair Warsh
items.sum: The FOMC voted 12-0 to hold the funds rate at 3.50%-3.75% at Kevin Warsh's first meeting as chair, but removed its prior cut bias and lifted the median end-2026 dot to 3.8%, with nine of eighteen participants projecting at least one hike. Seventeen of eighteen officials judged inflation risks tilted to the upside.
items.mkt: A hawkish hold is the dominant macro force: bearish for gold and commodities and supportive of a firm dollar and long USD/JPY carry near the 160 intervention line.

items.t: Dollar index eases to ~101.3 as traders trim Fed hike bets
items.sum: The DXY slipped to about 101.3 from a 14-month high near 101.8 hit on June 24, as cooler inflation signals led markets to scale back the magnitude of expected Fed hikes. The index is still up more than 2% on the month, its strongest monthly run since last July.
items.mkt: A firm but off-the-highs dollar keeps pressure on commodities and Asia FX while leaving room for relief in risk assets if Doha de-escalates.

items.t: Chinese Coast Guard keeps near-continuous patrols east of Taiwan
items.sum: Chinese Coast Guard vessels have patrolled waters east of Taiwan almost without pause since 1 June, with Beijing running a 'special maritime law enforcement operation' in response to Japan-Philippines efforts to delimit overlapping EEZ claims. Analysts read the activity as rehearsals for pressure or blockade.
items.mkt: Slow-burn second front. Not a mover yet, but the key Asia tail risk for TWD/Asia FX, TSMC and the Nikkei if it turns kinetic while the Middle East is unsettled.

items.t: Ukraine seizes momentum, raising its flag over the Kinburn Spit
items.sum: Ukrainian forces raised a flag over the Kinburn Spit for the first time since 2022, part of more than 480 square kilometres reclaimed since late January. With the Russian economy under strain, President Putin has hinted at peace talks even as missile and drone exchanges continue.
items.mkt: A second de-escalation thread in Europe trims one tail risk for energy and European assets, though any Russian escalation in response remains a watch item.
